title: South Carolina Pre-Calculus LO Mapping
subject: PreCalculus
method: Mathematical content alignment — each section's actual taught operations, structures,

sections.title: Exponents & Radicals
sections.textbook_content: Properties of integer/rational exponents · nth roots · simplifying radicals ·

primary_alignments.justification: Rational exponent expressions are a subclass of rational expressions; simplifying aᵐ/ⁿ = (ⁿ√a)ᵐ is precisely the rewriting operation this LO targets

supporting_alignments.justification: Students must interpret the structure of exponential and radical expressions to determine equivalence
Factoring out common radical/exponential factors requires recognizing structural patterns

sections.title: Rational Expressions
sections.textbook_content: Domain of algebraic expressions · simplifying rational expressions (cancel common

primary_alignments.justification: Direct match: the LO is the closed system of rational expressions under arithmetic operations — exactly what A.4 teaches (add, subtract, multiply, divide, simplify)
Simplifying rational expressions by factoring and canceling common polynomial factors is the rewriting operation PC.AAPR.6 defines

supporting_alignments.justification: Finding domain requires interpreting the structure of the denominator expression

sections.title: Solving Equations
sections.textbook_content: Linear equations · rational equations · quadratic equations (factoring, extracting

primary_alignments.justification: The quadratic-solving techniques here (all four methods) are the same algebraic tools used when solving the quadratic component of linear-quadratic systems
Solving equations of the form f(x) = g(x) algebraically is the algebraic complement to the graphical approach PC.AREI.11 formalizes

supporting_alignments.justification: The equation-solving framework (isolate, apply inverse operation) taught here is the same structure used in PC.FLQE.4 when solving aˣ = b

sections.title: Functions
sections.textbook_content: Definition of function · function notation f(x) · domain and range · implied domain

primary_alignments.justification: The section's explicit objectives include "find the domains of functions" — direct domain/range determination from algebraic expressions
Function notation, identifying inputs/outputs, and evaluating f(x) are foundational key-feature skills
The difference quotient [f(x+h)−f(x)]/h is a function-building operation; evaluating it requires constructing and simplifying a new function expression

supporting_alignments.justification: The difference quotient is mathematically equivalent to the average rate of change formula; the algebraic setup is established here

sections.title: Analyzing Graphs of Functions
sections.textbook_content: Vertical Line Test · zeros of functions algebraically · increasing/decreasing intervals

primary_alignments.justification: Identifying zeros, intervals of increase/decrease, relative extrema, and symmetry (even/odd) are all explicitly named key features
The section directly defines and computes average rate of change as [f(x₂)−f(x₁)]/(x₂−x₁), the slope of the secant line
Using zeros, monotonicity intervals, and extrema derived from the formula to inform the graph is the core skill of PC.FIF.7
Reading domain and range from graphs is a stated objective

sections.title: A Library of Parent Functions
sections.textbook_content: Linear, squaring, cubic, square root, reciprocal, absolute value, greatest integer,

primary_alignments.justification: Graphing each parent function from its formula — identifying domain, range, intercepts, and shape — is exactly what PC.FIF.7 requires
Domain, range, continuity, inc/dec behavior, and end behavior are the key features catalogued for each parent function
Explicit domain and range identification for every parent function type

sections.title: Transformations of Functions
sections.textbook_content: Vertical/horizontal shifts (h, k in f(x−h)+k) · reflections over x- and y-axis ·

primary_alignments.justification: This is the defining content of PC.FBF.3: the section teaches every transformation type (shift, reflect, stretch/shrink) as operations on a parent function f(x)
Reading a transformed equation and producing the correct graph is the output skill

supporting_alignments.justification: Transformations systematically alter key features (domain, range, intercepts, extrema); tracking those changes is part of the analysis

sections.title: Combinations & Composite Functions
sections.textbook_content: (f+g)(x), (f−g)(x), (fg)(x), (f/g)(x) · domain of arithmetic combinations ·

primary_alignments.justification: The section is the direct instructional content for PC.FBF.1: building new functions through arithmetic operations and composition
Finding the domain of f/g (exclude zeros of g) and f∘g (g(x) must be in domain of f) is a major focus

supporting_alignments.justification: Decomposing h(x) into f∘g requires interpreting the nested algebraic structure

sections.title: Inverse Functions
sections.textbook_content: Informal inverses · Horizontal Line Test (one-to-one functions) · algebraic procedure

primary_alignments.justification: Direct content match: the section teaches exactly what PC.FBF.4 requires — finding, verifying, interpreting, and graphing inverse functions
The algebraic and graphical inverse framework established here is the same framework applied to aˣ ↔ log_a(x) in PC.FBF.5

supporting_alignments.justification: The domain of f equals the range of f⁻¹ and vice versa — a key consequence analyzed here

sections.title: Polynomial & Synthetic Division
sections.textbook_content: Long division of polynomials · synthetic division (divisor x−k only) · Remainder

primary_alignments.justification: The section's three stated objectives map precisely to PC.AAPR.2: long division, synthetic division, Remainder Theorem, and Factor Theorem

supporting_alignments.justification: The Factor Theorem establishes the connection between zeros (roots) and factors — the foundation for finding x-intercepts when graphing
Verifying that f(x) = (x−k)q(x) + r is a polynomial identity is an application of PC.AAPR.4

sections.title: Rational Functions
sections.textbook_content: Domain of rational functions · vertical asymptotes (zeros of denominator) ·

primary_alignments.justification: The section uses rational expression operations (factoring, cancellation) to analyze holes and asymptotes — the graphical behavior of rational expression quotients
Factoring numerator and denominator to simplify f(x) = (x²−9)/((x−3)(x+1)) is precisely the rewriting operation in PC.AAPR.6
Vertical asymptotes, horizontal asymptotes, holes, intercepts, and end behavior are all key features of rational functions
Producing the graph from the rational formula by analyzing all features is the culminating skill

sections.title: Nonlinear Inequalities
sections.textbook_content: Polynomial inequalities (sign analysis with test intervals, key numbers) · rational

primary_alignments.justification: Sign analysis for f(x) > 0 or f(x) < g(x) uses the same graphical/algebraic intersection framework as PC.AREI.11
Finding the domain of √(64−4x²) requires solving a polynomial inequality — the direct application taught at section's end

supporting_alignments.justification: Rational inequalities require the same sign-analysis technique applied to rational expressions

sections.title: Exponential Functions & Their Graphs
sections.textbook_content: f(x) = aˣ with properties · natural base e · compound interest A=P(1+r/n)^(nt) ·

primary_alignments.justification: The section explicitly distinguishes exponential growth from linear growth and establishes the mathematical structure (constant ratio vs. constant difference)
Constructing A = Pert and A = P(1+r/n)^(nt) from contextual parameters is the function-building task PC.FLQE.2 requires
Comparing quarterly vs. monthly vs. continuous compounding is a direct comparison of exponential growth rates
P = principal, r = rate, t = time in A=Pert — each parameter has a specific mathematical role students must interpret

supporting_alignments.justification: Graphing f(x)=aˣ and its transformations from the formula

sections.title: Logarithmic Functions & Their Graphs
sections.textbook_content: log_a(x) as inverse of aˣ · common log (base 10) · natural log ln x (base e) ·

primary_alignments.justification: The section's core mathematical content is precisely PC.FBF.5: log_a(x) is defined as the inverse of aˣ, with graphical verification (reflection over y=x)
log_a(x) = y ↔ aʸ = x is the defining equivalence that makes logarithms the solution mechanism for exponential equations
Finding domain of ln(x−2), ln(2−x), ln(x²) requires the inequality-based domain analysis taught here

supporting_alignments.justification: In f(t)=75−6ln(t+1), the coefficient −6 and argument (t+1) have specific mathematical roles students interpret

sections.title: Properties of Logarithms
sections.textbook_content: Change-of-base formula log_a(x) = log(x)/log(a) · Product Property log_a(mn) =

primary_alignments.justification: The properties of logarithms are the algebraic tools that make it possible to isolate the exponent in aˣ = b — the manipulations PC.FLQE.4 requires
Using ln y = (3/2)ln x to derive y = x^(3/2) — interpreting logarithmic parameters to recover functional structure (planetary model)

supporting_alignments.justification: Expanding/condensing log expressions parallels simplifying rational expressions — both involve rewriting equivalent forms

sections.title: Exponential & Logarithmic Equations
sections.textbook_content: One-to-one property (aˣ=aʸ iff x=y) · solving by exponentiating both sides ·

primary_alignments.justification: The entire section is the computational execution of PC.FLQE.4: using logarithms to solve equations of the form aˣ = b, both with and without context
Solving −614+342.2 ln t = 240 to find when sales reach $240B requires interpreting what each parameter represents
The graphical verification (finding zeros of y = log 5x + log(x−1) − 2) is an instance of PC.AREI.11

sections.title: Exponential & Logarithmic Models
sections.textbook_content: Five model types — exponential growth (y=ae^(bx), b>0) · exponential decay

primary_alignments.justification: Building exponential and logarithmic models from real data (finding a and b) is the function-creation task in PC.FLQE.2
Each parameter in y=0.0033e^(−(x−511)²/28800) has a specific meaning (mean, spread) — the interpretation task of PC.FLQE.5
Selecting among growth, decay, logistic, and Gaussian models requires understanding when each structure is appropriate

sections.title: Radian & Degree Measure (+ Unit Circle intro)
sections.textbook_content: Radian measure definition · converting radians ↔ degrees · coterminal angles ·

primary_alignments.justification: The section is the definitional foundation for PC.FT.1: radian as ratio s/r, conversions, coterminal angles, and arc-length/sector-area applications
s=rθ and A=(1/2)r²θ are explicitly the formulas PC.GCI.5 requires students to apply in context

sections.title: Trig Functions: The Unit Circle
sections.textbook_content: Unit circle definition of sin t and cos t as (x,y) coordinates · all six trig functions

primary_alignments.justification: The section is the primary definitional source for PC.FT.2: sin and cos defined as coordinates on the unit circle for any real number t

primary_alignments.lo_name: Special Angles and the Unit Circle
primary_alignments.justification: Evaluating all six trig functions at multiples of π/6, π/4, π/3 is the exact computational task of PC.FT.3
Period=2π, the even property of cos, and the odd property of sin are the symmetry/periodicity properties PC.FT.4 formalizes

sections.title: Trig Functions of Any Angle
sections.textbook_content: Extension of trig definitions beyond the unit circle using (x,y,r) · reference angles ·

primary_alignments.justification: Extends the PC.FT.2 definition from the unit circle (r=1) to any angle with r=√(x²+y²) — a generalization of the same definition

primary_alignments.lo_name: Special Angles and the Unit Circle
primary_alignments.justification: Reference angle technique connects back to unit circle values at special angles
Using sin²θ+cos²θ=1 to find remaining trig functions from one given value — applying the Pythagorean identity algebraically

sections.title: Graphs of Sine & Cosine Functions
sections.textbook_content: Basic sine/cosine curves (5 key points per period) · amplitude |a| · period 2π/|b| ·

primary_alignments.justification: Writing y=a sin(bx−c)+d to model tidal data — fitting amplitude, period, phase shift, and vertical shift to real data — is the core task of PC.FT.5
The period formula 2π/b is a quantification of the periodicity property; amplitude and symmetry of the waveform are analyzed here
Producing the graph of a transformed sine/cosine from the equation using key-point analysis

sections.title: Graphs of Other Trig Functions
sections.textbook_content: Graph of tan x (period π, vertical asymptotes at π/2+nπ) · graph of cot x ·

primary_alignments.justification: Graphing tan, cot, sec, csc from their symbolic definitions — including asymptotes, period, range — is the PC.FIF.7 skill applied to these functions
Period π for tangent and cotangent, period 2π for secant and cosecant — periodicity analysis for the remaining four functions
Damped oscillation models g(x)·sin(x) extend periodic modeling to amplitude-varying phenomena

sections.title: Inverse Trig Functions
sections.textbook_content: Restricted domains for arcsin (−π/2,π/2), arccos (0,π), arctan (−π/2,π/2) ·

primary_alignments.justification: The section defines the three principal inverse trig functions with their restricted domains and ranges — the exact content of PC.FT.6
Using arcsin, arccos, arctan as computational tools to evaluate compositions (cos[arcsin(3/5)]) builds toward using them in equation-solving

supporting_alignments.justification: The domain-restriction strategy for creating invertible trig functions parallels the log/exp inverse relationship in PC.FBF.5

sections.title: Applications & Modeling
sections.textbook_content: Right-triangle applications (angle of elevation/depression, distance problems) ·

primary_alignments.justification: Simple harmonic motion d=a sin(ωt) is a periodic model with physical parameters (amplitude, period, frequency) — the applied modeling task of PC.FT.5
Solving for the angle of elevation (θ = arctan(height/distance)) uses inverse trig as the solution mechanism

supporting_alignments.justification: Right-triangle and bearing problems use the same geometric angle relationships as arc-length contexts

sections.title: Using Fundamental Identities
sections.textbook_content: Reciprocal identities (csc=1/sin, etc.) · quotient identities (tan=sin/cos) ·

primary_alignments.justification: The section is the primary instruction for PC.FT.8: establishing all fundamental identity families and training students to apply them algebraically to simplify and rewrite expressions

supporting_alignments.justification: Using identities to reduce trig equations to solvable forms is a prerequisite for PC.FT.7

sections.title: Verifying Trig Identities
sections.textbook_content: Strategy: work with one side only · algebraic techniques: factoring, combining

primary_alignments.justification: Verification is the proof-level engagement with PC.FT.8: students must demonstrate that two trig expressions are algebraically equivalent using identity chains

supporting_alignments.justification: The structure of verifying trig identities parallels verifying polynomial identities — both require algebraic manipulation to show equivalence

sections.title: Solving Trig Equations
sections.textbook_content: Collecting like terms (2sin x=1 → sin x=1/2) · factoring (2cos x(cos x+1)=0) ·

primary_alignments.justification: Solving trig equations requires applying inverse trig functions to isolate the angle — PC.FT.7 is the culminating LO this section builds toward
Squaring and converting to quadratic type requires substituting Pythagorean identities (sin²x = 1−cos²x) mid-solution
Graphical verification of solutions (intersection of y=sin x and y=1/2) is used to confirm algebraic solutions

sections.title: Sum & Difference Formulas
sections.textbook_content: sin(u±v)=sin u cos v ± cos u sin v · cos(u±v)=cos u cos v ∓ sin u sin v ·

primary_alignments.justification: The section is the direct instructional content for PC.FT.9: deriving, establishing, and applying all three sum/difference formula pairs
The cofunction identities (cos(π/2−x)=sin x) and reduction formulas derived here are instances of PC.FT.8 identities

supporting_alignments.justification: Sum/difference formulas are used to solve equations like sin[x+π/4]+sin[x−π/4]=−1

sections.title: Double & Half Angle Formulas
sections.textbook_content: Double-angle: sin(2u)=2sin u cos u; cos(2u)=cos²u−sin²u=1−2sin²u=2cos²u−1;

primary_alignments.justification: Double-angle formulas are direct corollaries of the sum formulas (sin(2u)=sin(u+u)) — they are derived from PC.FT.9 content and extended here
Power-reducing and half-angle formulas are further identities in the PC.FT.8 family; the section expands the identity toolkit
The projectile model r=(v₀²/32)sin(2θ) is a periodic function of launch angle — an applied modeling instance of PC.FT.5

sections.title: Law of Sines
sections.textbook_content: Oblique triangles (no right angle) · Law of Sines: (sin A)/a=(sin B)/b=(sin C)/c ·

primary_alignments.justification: The Law of Sines is one of the two laws named in PC.GSRT.10; the section provides full derivation and application
Using Law of Sines to find unknown sides and angles in oblique triangles is the direct application of PC.GSRT.11
A=(1/2)bc sin A is the non-right-triangle area formula explicitly targeted by PC.GSRT.9

sections.title: Law of Cosines
sections.textbook_content: Law of Cosines: a²=b²+c²−2bc cos A · solving SSS and SAS cases ·

primary_alignments.justification: The Law of Cosines is the second law in PC.GSRT.10; together with 6.1 this section completes the standard
SSS and SAS cases — determining all missing measurements of an oblique triangle — is the application PC.GSRT.11 requires
Heron's Formula is an alternative area formula for triangles given three sides; both PC.GSRT.9 formulas are taught in 6.1 and 6.2

sections.title: Vectors in the Plane
sections.textbook_content: Vectors as directed line segments · component form ⟨a,b⟩ · magnitude |v|=√(a²+b²) ·

primary_alignments.justification: Distinguishing vector (magnitude+direction) from scalar quantities is the entry-level conceptual task of PC.NVMQ.1
Writing vectors in component form ⟨a,b⟩ and converting from magnitude-direction form is the direct content of PC.NVMQ.2
Vector addition, subtraction, and scalar multiplication are the operations PC.NVMQ.4 requires
The section explicitly teaches scalar multiplication cv=c⟨a,b⟩=⟨ca,cb⟩
Force decomposition and velocity resultant problems are the modeling applications of PC.NVMQ.3

sections.title: Vectors & Dot Products
sections.textbook_content: Dot product u·v=a₁a₂+b₁b₂ · properties of dot product · angle between vectors:

primary_alignments.justification: The dot product is a vector operation; the section extends the operation set from 6.3 to include this inner product
Work as a dot product W=F·d is a direct physical modeling application of vector quantities

supporting_alignments.justification: Determining orthogonality (u·v=0) requires conceptual understanding of vector relationships

sections.title: The Complex Plane
sections.textbook_content: Plotting complex numbers a+bi as points (a,b) · absolute value |z|=√(a²+b²) ·

primary_alignments.justification: The section is the direct content for PC.NCNS.4: plotting complex numbers and their conjugates in the complex plane
Computing the complex conjugate and modulus are two of the three operations named in PC.NCNS.3
Addition of complex numbers interpreted as vector addition in the complex plane is the geometric content of PC.NCNS.5

sections.title: Trig Form of a Complex Number
sections.textbook_content: Modulus r=|z|=√(a²+b²) · argument θ · trigonometric form z=r(cos θ+i sin θ) ·

primary_alignments.justification: Multiplying complex numbers in trig form (multiply moduli, add angles) has a direct geometric interpretation (rotation+scaling) — the content of PC.NCNS.5
Multiplying and dividing complex numbers in trig form extends the arithmetic operations of PC.NCNS.2 to polar form
Division in trig form (divide moduli, subtract arguments) is the quotient operation of PC.NCNS.3 in polar representation

primary_alignments.lo_name: Polynomial Identities in the Complex Numbers
primary_alignments.justification: DeMoivre's Theorem zⁿ=rⁿ(cos nθ+i sin nθ) is a polynomial identity extended to complex numbers

sections.title: Linear & Nonlinear Systems
sections.textbook_content: Solving systems by substitution · solving systems by graphing · solving linear-quadratic

primary_alignments.justification: The section explicitly covers solving systems where one equation is linear and one is quadratic — the exact content of PC.AREI.7
Using graphs to find and verify intersection points of any two functions f and g is the graphical method of PC.AREI.11

sections.title: Two-Variable Linear Systems
sections.textbook_content: Method of elimination (multiply + add to eliminate one variable) · consistent/dependent/

primary_alignments.justification: The 2×2 linear system solved here is precisely the type represented as AX=B in PC.AREI.8; the elimination method is the algebraic precursor
Graphical interpretation of consistent/inconsistent/dependent systems uses the f(x)=g(x) intersection framework

sections.title: Matrices & Systems of Equations
sections.textbook_content: Matrix notation · augmented matrix [A|b] · three elementary row operations ·

primary_alignments.justification: Writing a system of equations as an augmented matrix [A
Using matrices to organize and solve systems of equations (including data-fitting to find a quadratic model) is the application content of PC.NVMQ.6
Gauss-Jordan elimination on [A

sections.title: Operations with Matrices
sections.textbook_content: Matrix equality · matrix addition and subtraction · scalar multiplication · matrix

primary_alignments.justification: Addition, subtraction, scalar multiplication, and matrix multiplication are all named in PC.NVMQ.7
Demonstrating that AB≠BA (non-commutativity) while (AB)C=A(BC) (associativity) holds — the specific properties PC.NVMQ.8 targets
Using matrix multiplication to transform a vector v: the section explicitly teaches Av as a vector transformation
The transformation examples (using specific 2×2 matrices to transform geometric vectors) are instances of PC.NVMQ.11
Matrix representation of AX=B links the data-organization function of matrices to their algebraic role

sections.title: Inverse of a Square Matrix
sections.textbook_content: Definition of inverse: AA⁻¹=A⁻¹A=I · Gauss-Jordan method to find A⁻¹ (row-reduce [A|I]→[I|A⁻¹]) ·

primary_alignments.justification: The section is the direct instructional content for PC.AREI.9: finding inverse matrices and using them to solve linear systems
The identity matrix I, the zero matrix O, the definition of A⁻¹, and the singular (non-invertible) matrix are the three entities of PC.NVMQ.9

sections.title: Determinant of a Square Matrix
sections.textbook_content: det([a b; c d])=ad−bc · minors M_ij · cofactors C_ij=(−1)^(i+j)M_ij ·

primary_alignments.justification: The invertibility condition det(A)≠0 directly links the determinant to the existence of A⁻¹ — a core relationship in PC.NVMQ.9
Computing the determinant is an operation on a matrix; cofactor expansion is a structured computational procedure

supporting_alignments.justification: det([a b; c d])=ad−bc is an algebraic identity; cofactor expansion expresses the determinant as a polynomial identity in the matrix entries

sections.title: Applications of Matrices & Determinants
sections.textbook_content: Cramer's Rule: x=det(Aₓ)/det(A) · area of triangle using determinant ·

primary_alignments.justification: Cramer's Rule is an alternative determinant-based method to solve AX=B — a direct application of PC.AREI.9's goal (solving systems via matrix methods)
Using 2×2 matrices to transform geometric regions and compute areas of parallelograms is precisely the content of PC.NVMQ.11
The encoding/decoding cryptography application (multiply row vectors by matrix A and A⁻¹) is a vector transformation in the sense of PC.NVMQ.10

sections.title: Parabolas
sections.textbook_content: Conic sections as plane-cone intersections · standard form of parabola x²=4py

primary_alignments.justification: The section's mathematical content is exactly PC.GGPE.2: deriving the standard form x²=4py from the geometric locus definition (equidistance from focus and directrix)

sections.title: Ellipses
sections.textbook_content: Ellipse defined as locus where sum of distances from two foci is constant ·

primary_alignments.justification: The section derives the standard ellipse equation from the geometric locus definition (constant sum of focal distances) — exactly what PC.GGPE.3 requires for ellipses

sections.title: Hyperbolas
sections.textbook_content: Hyperbola defined as locus where difference of distances from two foci is constant ·

primary_alignments.justification: The section derives the standard hyperbola equation from the geometric locus definition (constant difference of focal distances) — the hyperbola component of PC.GGPE.3

sections.title: Polar Coordinates
sections.textbook_content: Polar coordinate system (r,θ) — pole and polar axis · plotting polar points ·

primary_alignments.justification: The conversions x=r cos θ and y=r sin θ apply the unit-circle definitions of cosine and sine — polar coordinates are a direct application of PC.FT.2

supporting_alignments.justification: The polar angle θ is measured in radians; the coordinate system requires fluency with PC.FT.1

sections.title: Graphs of Polar Equations
sections.textbook_content: Symmetry tests for polar graphs (about polar axis, θ=π/2, and pole) · graphing by

primary_alignments.justification: Producing the graph of a polar equation by analyzing symmetry, zeros, and maximum r-values from the symbolic form is the graphing skill of PC.FIF.7 applied in polar coordinates
Rose curves r=a sin(nθ) are periodic in θ; symmetry tests (polar axis, θ=π/2, pole) are applications of the symmetry concepts in PC.FT.4

uncovered_los.name: Graphing Polynomial Functions
uncovered_los.reason: Sections 2.1–2.2 (not in pacing guide) cover polynomial graphs; only 2.3 is included

uncovered_los.name: Polynomial Identities
uncovered_los.reason: No section is dedicated to proving polynomial identities (e.g., sum of cubes, difference of squares as identity)

uncovered_los.name: Binomial Theorem
uncovered_los.reason: Section 9.5 is not in the pacing guide

uncovered_los.name: Quadratics with Complex Solutions
uncovered_los.reason: Covered in Section 2.4 (not in pacing guide)
